methodologies.name: Orchestration Pattern Selection
methodologies.summary: Before you wire agents together, decide how they will be coordinated. There are two families. In a Workflow the steps are fixed and you can see the control flow, such as run in order, branch on a condition, or fan out in parallel. In an Autonomous setup the agents decide the flow themselves, such as planning, handing off, or chatting as a group. Compare the two on a few things: how predictable they are, how fast, how easy to observe, how much they cost, and how they tend to fail. Pick the simplest one that does the job. If neither family fits on its own, mix them. You can wrap a fixed workflow around an autonomous core, or drop autonomous agents inside workflow stages. The common mistake is to skip this comparison and reach straight for full autonomy. That gives you an agent graph nobody can watch or debug.
methodologies.intent: Make a deliberate choice between a fixed workflow and a self-directing setup, judged against named criteria, before any agent graph is written in code.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when you design a multi-agent system from scratch. Also use it when an existing system fails in ways its current coordination cannot handle. Apply it when you can name the task but cannot yet defend one topology over the others. Don't apply it for a single agent with one tool, because there is no coordination choice to make. One exception: if your platform forces one family, such as a strict workflow engine or a swarm-only runtime, then you only choose within that family.

inputs.name: Task decomposition
inputs.description: The user-facing task broken down into jobs that single agents could own.

inputs.name: Operating constraints
inputs.description: The limits you have to live with. How much latency you can spend, how much cost you can spend, what you need to be able to observe, and how much unpredictability you can accept.

inputs.name: Candidate patterns
inputs.description: The set of coordination shapes you can choose from, such as run in order, branch, run in parallel, plan-and-execute, hand off, group chat, or a mix.

outputs.name: Selected orchestration pattern
outputs.description: The chosen pattern, with its family stated outright as Workflow or Autonomous.

outputs.name: Selection rationale
outputs.description: A written case for the choice. It scores the chosen pattern against the alternatives on each criterion.

outputs.name: Hybrid composition (optional)
outputs.description: If neither family fits alone, the exact way you combined them. For example, workflow stages wrapping a self-directing sub-team.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  start[Task decomposition + constraints] --> classify[Classify candidate topologies]
  classify --> wf{Workflow family fits?}
  wf -- yes --> wfpick[Pick sequential / conditional / parallel]
  wf -- no --> auto{Autonomous family fits?}
  auto -- yes --> autopick[Pick plan-execute / handoff / group-chat]
  auto -- no --> hybrid[Compose hybrid: workflow shell over autonomous core]
  wfpick --> score[Score against predictability, latency, observability, cost]
  autopick --> score
  hybrid --> score
  score --> pick[Apply selection criteria: weakest topology that solves]
  pick --> doc[Document rejected alternatives]
  doc --> out[Selected orchestration + rationale]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A platform team at a B2B analytics company is wiring up an internal 'data question' assistant. Here is the task. A user asks a plain-language question about their warehouse. The system fetches the right slice of schema, drafts SQL, runs it against a sandboxed replica, and returns a chart with prose. The team's instinct was 'multi-agent group chat with a planner, an analyst, and a charter agent.'

Applying the methodology, they classified that candidate as Autonomous (group-chat). When they scored it, group-chat lost on predictability, because the planner kept reopening decisions. It also lost on observability, because there was no clean per-stage trace. A workflow alternative ran the steps in order: schema-fetch, then SQL-draft, then SQL-execute, then chart-render. It won on every criterion except the rare 'multi-warehouse join' query class. They picked the workflow as the main topology. They added a single autonomous escape hatch that the planner could trigger for joins. They documented the mix: a workflow shell, with a self-directing sub-team called only for the join class.

What they learned: writing down the rejected group-chat design, and the criteria it lost on, mattered. Four months later it stopped a junior engineer from 'simplifying things by going group-chat again.' The reasoning, not the topology, was the lasting artefact. Per-stage P95 latency dropped from 38s to 11s once they stopped paying for emergent coordination on every query.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The two families map to a control-flow axis. **Workflow (explicit control)** covers sequential, conditional, and parallel orchestration — the control flow is statically authored and the agent graph is fixed at design time. **Autonomous (emergent control)** covers plan-and-execute, handoff, and group-chat — the control flow is decided at runtime by the agents themselves.

Selection runs against a fixed criteria set:

- **Predictability** — workflow topologies are deterministic in structure; autonomous topologies admit run-to-run variance (the planner may relitigate, group-chat may not converge).
- **Latency** — emergent coordination adds round-trips per query; a fixed pipeline pays only for its declared stages.
- **Observability** — workflow gives a clean per-stage trace; autonomous graphs often have no stable stage boundary to instrument.
- **Cost** — autonomy spends tokens on coordination overhead on every invocation.
- **Failure-mode shape** — workflow fails predictably at a known stage; autonomous fails diffusely (non-convergence, bottlenecked orchestrator, hero-agent overload).

The operative rule is *weakest-topology-that-solves*: pick the most constrained, most explicit shape that still covers the task, because autonomy is paid for in observability and guardrails. **Hybrids** are first-class named compositions — a workflow shell wrapping an autonomous core, or autonomous segments interleaved between workflow stages — never undocumented escape hatches. Each selection writes down its rejected alternatives and the criteria they lost on; the rationale is the durable artefact, not the topology.

steps.name: Classify candidate orchestrations into the taxonomy
steps.description: Tag each plausible topology. It is Workflow if the steps are fixed: run in order, branch, or run in parallel. It is Autonomous if the agents decide the flow: plan, hand off, or group chat. If a topology cannot be tagged, it is too vague. Go back and break it down further.

steps.name: Score against comparative criteria
steps.description: Score each candidate on five things: how predictable it is, how fast, how easy to observe, how much it costs, and how it tends to fail. Workflow usually wins on predictability and on being easy to watch. Autonomous usually wins on flexibility and on handling tasks you did not script.

steps.name: Apply selection criteria
steps.description: Pick the most constrained, most explicit shape that still solves the task. Autonomy costs you in observability and in guardrails. Only buy as much of it as the task actually needs.

steps.name: Compose hybrids when no single pattern fits
steps.description: Sometimes you need both predictability and the ability to handle unscripted tasks. Then wrap a self-directing sub-team inside a fixed workflow, or slot workflow stages between autonomous segments. That combination becomes its own named topology.

steps.name: Document the rejected alternatives
steps.description: Record what you considered and why you turned it down. The next team inherits both the choice and the reasoning. Without the reasoning, the design drifts back to whatever feels fashionable.

principles: Pick the most constrained coordination that still solves the task. You pay for autonomy in observability.
Hybrids are named, documented combinations. They are not undocumented escape hatches.
Every choice writes down what it rejected. Without that, the choice cannot be audited.
Workflow and Autonomous are families, not answers. Never pick 'multi-agent' without naming the actual pattern.

known_failure_modes.note: Reaching for the orchestrator-workers pattern by default until the orchestrator becomes a serial chokepoint for every multi-agent decision.
Selecting an autonomous topology when a workflow would suffice, then loading one agent with every responsibility.
Choosing a long sequential workflow without budgeting for multiplicative per-step error.

sources.quote: Part I: Foundations — Ch 1 Understanding Multi-Agent Systems, Ch 2 Multi-Agent Patterns, Ch 3 UX Design Principles

methodologies.name: Protocol Selection: MCP Vs A2A
methodologies.summary: When agents run in separate places, you need a protocol for them to talk over the wire. Pick it by looking at the shape of each connection. Use MCP when a host agent needs clean, typed access to tools and data sources. MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. Use A2A when separate agents work together as peers, especially across team or trust boundaries. A2A stands for Agent-to-Agent. Use both when one agent is at once a tool user and a peer. Make security the deciding factor, not which protocol you read about first. Look at what credentials are exposed, how the connection is encrypted, and how user identity is passed along.
methodologies.intent: Pick the right wire protocol, MCP or A2A or both, by looking at the shape of each connection and its security needs, rather than defaulting to whatever is trending.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when an agent has to talk to external tools, data, or other agents across a process boundary or an organisational boundary. Apply it before you open a port or publish a manifest. Don't apply it when everything runs in one process, because direct function calls are simpler and avoid the protocol overhead. One exception: a single-process system that expects to split soon may still gain from drawing the protocol seam early.

inputs.name: Integration inventory
inputs.description: A list of every external system the agent must talk to. Cover tools, data sources, and peer agents. For each one, note who owns it and where the trust boundaries are.

inputs.name: Trust map
inputs.description: For each connection, which ones cross an organisational boundary, which ones carry user credentials, and which ones are first-party.

inputs.name: Latency and throughput requirements
inputs.description: For each connection, how fast each call has to be and how much total traffic it will see.

outputs.name: Per-integration protocol assignment
outputs.description: Each connection tagged MCP, A2A, both, or 'direct'. Direct means no protocol because it runs in-process.

outputs.name: Security posture per protocol
outputs.description: For each protocol seam: how identity is passed along, how the connection is encrypted, what the credentials can reach, and where the audit hook is.

outputs.name: Composition note
outputs.description: Where MCP and A2A are used together, a clear note saying which agent plays which role at the seam.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  inv[Integration inventory + trust map] --> classify{Per integration: shape?}
  classify -- consumes tool/data --> mcp[Assign MCP]
  classify -- peer agent collab --> a2a[Assign A2A]
  classify -- in-process --> direct[Direct call, no protocol]
  classify -- both roles --> both[Assign MCP + A2A]
  mcp --> sec{Security gate: credentials, TLS, audit, identity?}
  a2a --> sec
  both --> sec
  sec -- pass --> pin[Pin protocol versions + capability manifests]
  sec -- fail --> reconsider[Reconsider protocol or harden seam]
  reconsider --> classify
  pin --> rec[Record rejected alternatives]
  rec --> out[Per-integration protocol assignment + security posture]

methodologies.example_scenario:
An engineering team at a logistics SaaS is adding an 'ops copilot' agent. It talks to internal services and to a partner's pricing-optimisation agent. Their integration inventory: a Postgres warehouse, an internal route-planner API, a partner-hosted pricing agent, and a shared S3 bucket of historical shipments. Their trust map: the warehouse and route-planner are first-party, the pricing agent crosses an organisational boundary, and S3 is first-party but holds customer data.

Applying the methodology, the warehouse and route-planner became MCP servers. They are typed tools the copilot uses on behalf of a signed-in user, with user identity passed along as an OAuth claim. The pricing-agent connection became A2A. It used mTLS, a capability manifest pinned at v0.3, and a deliberate scope-down so the partner only sees an opaque task ID and aggregate parameters. The S3 bucket became MCP too, but routed through a thin server that enforces a per-customer prefix. The copilot itself plays both roles. It is an MCP client for the warehouse, route-planner, and S3. It is an A2A peer when it offloads pricing optimisation.

What they learned: writing down the rejected option saved them later. That option was 'expose route-planner via A2A because we already had A2A client code.' Six weeks on, the partner's A2A library shipped a breaking change. The team had a working MCP path the whole time. The security gate also caught two seams where the credentials were effectively root. Both got scoped down before launch.
methodologies.deep_dive:
The two protocols occupy different lanes. **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** is the host↔tool/data lane: a host agent consumes typed tools and data sources, typically on behalf of an authenticated user, with identity propagated as a claim (e.g. OAuth). **A2A (Agent-to-Agent)** is the agent↔agent lane: peers exchange task delegation, capability discovery, and opaque sub-task offload across organisational or trust boundaries. An agent that is simultaneously a tool consumer and a peer speaks both.

The security gate is the decisive filter, evaluated per seam before adoption:

- **Credential scope** — what the credentials at the seam can reach; root-equivalent scope is a failure condition.
- **Identity propagation** — how user identity flows across the seam (OAuth claim for MCP-on-behalf-of-user; scoped task identity for A2A).
- **Transport** — TLS for first-party MCP, mTLS for cross-boundary A2A.
- **Audit hook** — where the seam is instrumented for traceability.

A protocol that satisfies the functional need but fails the security posture is rejected regardless of convenience or fashion. **Capability manifests and protocol versions are pinned artefacts** — e.g. an A2A manifest pinned at a fixed version — so upgrades become documented events rather than silent breaks. Cross-boundary A2A seams should be scoped down to expose only opaque identifiers and aggregate parameters. Composition (MCP + A2A on the same agent) is normal and must document the role played at each port.

steps.name: Map each integration to a protocol family
steps.description: If the agent uses a tool or data source on behalf of a user, that seam wants MCP. If the agent works with a peer agent, that seam wants A2A. Peer work means things like handing off a task, discovering what another agent can do, or passing off an opaque sub-task. Some seams want both.

steps.name: Apply security considerations as a gate
steps.description: Before you adopt a protocol on a seam, name four things. What the credentials can reach. How user identity is passed along. How the connection is encrypted, such as TLS or mTLS. And where the audit hook is. A protocol that does the job but cannot meet the security bar is rejected, no matter how fashionable it is.

steps.name: Compose MCP and A2A where the agent is both consumer and peer
steps.description: An agent can offer its own abilities to other agents over A2A while using third-party tools over MCP. Then it speaks both. Write down the shape of each seam so the role at every port is clear.

steps.name: Pin protocol versions and capability manifests
steps.description: Record the version of each protocol you use, and the capability manifest each agent or tool advertises. That way an upgrade is a documented event, not a silent break.

steps.name: Record the rejected alternatives
steps.description: When you pick MCP over A2A for a seam, or the other way round, write down why. Six months later the integration breaks. The reasoning is what stops a blind switch.

principles: MCP is for host-to-tool and host-to-data. A2A is for agent-to-agent. Do not stretch either one into the other's lane.
Security gates the protocol choice. Doing the job is not enough on its own.
Using MCP and A2A together is normal. Just document the role at each seam.
Capability manifests and protocol versions are pinned on purpose. They are not loose defaults.

known_failure_modes.note: Picking a protocol because it's trending without locking down transport, identity propagation, and credential scope.
Adopting MCP without curating the tool surface — every new server adds dozens of tools the model now has to disambiguate.
Picking a protocol because a single vendor's SDK is convenient, then discovering the wire format embeds vendor-specific extensions.

sources.title: Model Context Protocol — specification
sources.quote: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools.

methodologies.name: Writer-Critic Iterative Loop Construction
methodologies.summary: Build a loop with two agents. One agent produces something, such as code, a plan, a summary, or a draft. A separate agent checks it against a clear pass/fail rubric. They go back and forth until the checker passes or you hit a set number of rounds. This is how you build the generator-critic-separation pattern. It says how to wire the pair together, what the rubric has to contain, and how to cap the loop so it cannot run forever.
methodologies.intent: Wire a maker agent and a checker agent into a loop with a clear rubric and a hard round limit, so quality climbs through bounded review instead of a single shot.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when the output has checkable quality. For example, code that must pass tests, a plan that must meet named constraints, or a summary that must cover set points. And use it when one shot is not good enough. Apply it when you can actually write the rubric down. Don't apply it when 'good' is purely a matter of taste and the rubric boils down to 'the user likes it'. At that point you need a person in the loop, not a checker agent. One exception: even taste-based tasks can use a checker if you can spell out what to avoid.

inputs.name: Task specification
inputs.description: What the maker must produce. Include the input format and what counts as success.

inputs.name: Pass/fail rubric
inputs.description: The checklist the checker applies. It must be clear enough that two sensible checkers would reach the same verdict.

inputs.name: Maximum iteration count
inputs.description: A hard limit on the number of maker-checker rounds, usually 3 to 8. Past that limit the loop stops and returns the best draft so far.

outputs.name: Generator agent
outputs.description: The agent that produces the output and takes in the checker's feedback on later rounds.

outputs.name: Critic agent
outputs.description: The agent that applies the rubric and returns pass or fail with structured feedback. It never quietly rewrites the output itself.

outputs.name: Loop controller
outputs.description: The driver that connects maker and checker, enforces the round limit, and returns the best output if the loop runs out of rounds.

diagram.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as Caller
  participant L as Loop controller
  participant W as Writer (generator)
  participant C as Critic (judge)
  U->>L: task spec + rubric + max_iters
  L->>W: generate(task spec)
  W-->>L: draft 1
  loop until pass or cap
    L->>C: judge(draft, rubric)
    alt pass
      C-->>L: pass
    else fail with hints
      C-->>L: fail + actionable hints
      L->>W: revise(draft, hints)
      W-->>L: next draft
    end
  end
  L-->>U: passing artefact or best-so-far

methodologies.example_scenario:
A small platform team is building an internal 'release-notes drafter' agent. It reads a list of merged PRs and produces release notes the docs team will accept without rewriting. A single shot gave drafts that were technically correct but wrong in tone, and the docs team rewrote 80% of them. The team decided to wire a maker-checker loop.

They wrote the rubric first, before either agent existed. Every entry must do four things. Name the user-facing capability, not the internal module. Link to the relevant PR. Avoid 'refactor' and 'improvement' as standalone bullets. And stay under 25 words. The verdict had three shapes: pass, fail-with-hints, and fail-terminally. The maker was Sonnet with a release-notes system prompt. The checker was a different model, GPT-4.1, with a strict rubric-only prompt that emits structured JSON. The round limit was 4, keeping the highest-rated draft if the loop ran out.

To calibrate, they ran 30 historical PR lists through the loop and had the docs lead grade the results. The checker agreed with the docs lead 26 times out of 30. The 4 disagreements traced to two unclear rubric items, which they tightened. After launch, 70% of notes pass on round 2, 22% on round 3, and 8% hit the limit. The ones that hit the limit were almost all huge PR batches where the rubric could not be met in 25 words, so they added a 'split batch' path. They had earlier had a sycophancy bug where the checker praised drafts just for matching tone. Without the round limit, that bug would have looped forever. With the limit, the worst case was a slightly-off draft, not an outage.
methodologies.deep_dive:
This methodology constructs the **generator-critic-separation** pattern as a bounded iterative loop. Three roles, never collapsed:

- **Generator** — produces the artefact on turn one from the task spec alone; on later turns it consumes the critic's structured feedback and revises. It never grades itself.
- **Critic** — applies the rubric and emits a structured verdict (`pass` / `fail-with-hints` / `fail-terminally`). On fail it returns the smallest actionable hint, not a rewrite. Same-model self-critique ceilings at the model's own blind spots, so use a distinct model or, at minimum, a hard role-separating prompt.
- **Loop controller** — alternates the two, terminates on pass or at the iteration cap (typically 3–8), and on exhaustion surfaces the highest-rated draft. It emits a per-iteration trace for retrospective audit.

Calibration is mandatory: run a sample through critic and human grader, then tighten the rubric until verdict agreement is acceptable. A miscalibrated critic either over-passes (false confidence) or over-fails (loop exhaustion). Instrument iteration counts, exit reasons, and rubric-pass rates per task class. The characteristic failure modes are **same-model-self-critique** (verdicts collapse to 'the generator already thought this was good'), **unbounded-loop** (no cap or best-so-far fallback turns an unsatisfiable rubric into an outage), and **sycophancy** (critic rewards tone agreement over rubric application, inflating pass rates).

steps.name: Author the rubric the critic will apply
steps.description: Write the rubric down before either agent exists. List what to check, the verdict shape (pass/fail or a score), and what counts as 'fail with hints' versus 'fail terminally'. A rubric written after the maker is skewed toward what the maker already does well.

steps.name: Build the generator agent
steps.description: On the first turn the maker produces the output from the task spec alone. On later turns it takes in the checker's structured feedback. The maker never grades itself.

steps.name: Build the critic agent with role separation
steps.description: The checker only judges. It does not produce the output. It returns a structured verdict, and on a fail it returns the smallest useful hint that would let the maker improve. If one model both makes and checks, the two roles collapse into one agent. Use a different model, or at least a prompt that hard-separates the roles.

steps.name: Wire the bounded loop
steps.description: The loop controller alternates maker and checker. It passes when the checker passes. It stops at the round limit and returns the highest-rated output. It emits a trace for each round so you can audit the loop afterward.

steps.name: Calibrate the rubric against ground truth
steps.description: Run a sample of outputs through both the checker and a human grader. Tighten the rubric until the checker's verdicts agree with the human at an acceptable rate. A poorly calibrated checker either passes too much, which gives false confidence, or fails too much, which exhausts the loop.

steps.name: Instrument failure modes
steps.description: Log round counts, exit reasons, and pass rates per task type. Watch for task types where the loop nearly always runs out of rounds. That signals the rubric cannot be met, or the maker cannot act on the checker's hints.

principles: The checker and the maker are separate roles. One model doing both ceilings at that model's blind spots.
Freeze the rubric before you build either agent. A rubric written afterward is tainted by what the maker already does well.
Every loop has a hard round limit and a 'best so far' fallback. An open-ended loop is an outage waiting to happen.
On a fail, the checker returns useful hints, not just a verdict. The maker has to be able to revise on the next turn.

known_failure_modes.note: Generator and critic share the model and prompt family — verdicts collapse to 'the generator already thought this was good.'
No iteration cap or 'best so far' fallback — the loop runs forever when the rubric is unsatisfiable.
Critic rewards agreement with the generator's tone instead of applying the rubric — inflated pass rates.

sources.title: AutoGen — companion framework cited throughout Lanham Ch 4 (writer + critic example)
sources.quote: A framework for creating multi-agent AI applications that can act autonomously or work alongside humans.

methodologies.name: AOSE Lifecycle Methodology (Prometheus-style)
methodologies.summary: This is a full, step-by-step process for building a multi-agent system, adapted from Prometheus and related methods. It runs in phases: gather requirements, write the system spec, design the architecture, design each agent in detail, build it, test it, then maintain it. Each phase produces a named document that feeds the next. The agents are described in terms of what they believe, what they want, and what they plan to do (the formal name is BDI, for Belief-Desire-Intention). This is the heavyweight opposite of 'wire up an agent loop and iterate'. People have used it on long-lived multi-agent systems for decades. It trades speed for a clear trail from each goal to the agent that ends up running in production.
methodologies.intent: Take a multi-agent system from rough requirements to a maintained production deployment along a fully traceable path, with named documents at each phase that outlast staff turnover.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this for a long-lived multi-agent system where the trail from goal to agent matters more than a fast first demo. That covers safety-critical agents, regulated domains, multi-team systems where the contracts between agents must be auditable, and research deployments meant to outlast the original team. Don't apply it for a throwaway prototype, a single agent loop, or a system whose requirements you expect to rewrite every week. The overhead is wasted there. One exception: even small projects can borrow a single artefact, such as a goal hierarchy or a role model, without taking on the whole process.

inputs.name: Stakeholder goals
inputs.description: What the people who rely on the system need, stated informally. What it should achieve, who depends on it, and what success looks like.

inputs.name: Domain knowledge
inputs.description: The subject-matter expertise needed to model the world the agents work in. The things in it, how it changes, the constraints, and what the agents can sense.

inputs.name: Available agent technology
inputs.description: What the build will run on. The agent platform, the way agents communicate, the agent toolkit, and the runtime environment.

outputs.name: System specification
outputs.description: The formal statement of what the system must do. It holds the goal hierarchy, the scenarios, the actions the agents can take, and what they can sense.

outputs.name: Architectural design
outputs.description: The agent types, who plays which role, how the agents talk to each other, and what data they share.

outputs.name: Detailed agent designs
outputs.description: For each agent: what it can do, its plans, what it believes about the world, and how it handles events.

outputs.name: Implemented multi-agent system
outputs.description: Running code that turns the architecture into reality on the chosen agent platform.

outputs.name: Test artefacts and maintenance plan
outputs.description: Tests for single agents and for how they work together, plus a written procedure for changing the system over time.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  goals[Stakeholder goals + domain knowledge] --> req[Requirements analysis]
  req --> spec[System specification: goal hierarchy, scenarios, percepts/actions]
  spec --> arch[Architectural design: agent types, roles, protocols, shared data]
  arch --> det[Detailed agent design: BDI plans, beliefs, capabilities, events]
  det --> impl[Implementation on BDI platform]
  impl --> test[Testing: unit, integration, model-check key properties]
  test --> deploy[Deployed multi-agent system]
  deploy --> maint[Maintenance: walk req-goal-role-agent-plan chain on change]
  maint -.requirement shift.-> req

methodologies.example_scenario:
A research group inside a national air-traffic authority is building a long-lived multi-agent decision-support system for runway-incursion advisories. The system must outlive the original team, pass external audit, and stay patchable a decade out. They adopt the Prometheus-style lifecycle because lightweight 'wire up an agent loop' approaches would not survive the audit-trail requirement.

Requirements analysis produces a goal hierarchy. The root goal is 'advise controllers on incursion risk'. It breaks down into sub-goals for detection, prioritisation, advisory generation, and acknowledgement tracking. The system specification names what the agents can sense (the radar feed, ground-vehicle GPS, and a voice-transcript stream) and what they can do (push an advisory, log a decision, escalate). Architectural design groups the goals into four agent types: Detector, Prioritiser, Advisor, and Logger. It defines explicit FIPA-style protocols between them. Detailed design specifies each agent's plans, beliefs, and event handlers. For example, Advisor.draft-advisory uses a context-aware plan with a fallback recipe, and one belief is current-runway-state. Implementation targets the Jadex platform. Testing combines unit tests for plans, integration tests for the protocols, and model-checking liveness for the escalation protocol.

What they learned: two years in, regulators demanded a new advisory class, and the maintenance discipline paid for itself. The change walked cleanly from a new requirement, through a new sub-goal, into the Prioritiser's plan library, with audit-trail documents at every step. A separate team in the same authority had built a similar system as a single LLM agent loop. When their regulator asked the same question, they could not show what had changed or why, and they rebuilt from scratch.
methodologies.deep_dive:
This is **AOSE (agent-oriented software engineering)** in the Prometheus / Winikoff-Padgham tradition, organised around **BDI (Belief-Desire-Intention)** agents: *beliefs* model the agent's view of the world, *desires* are the goals, and *intentions* are the plans currently being executed. The lifecycle phases and their artefacts:

- **Requirements analysis** → a goal hierarchy (parent goals decomposed to actionable leaves) plus scenarios and environment characteristics.
- **System specification** → the system boundary: percepts (inputs the agents receive), actions (outputs they can take), and scenarios linking goals to action sequences; a system overview diagram.
- **Architectural design** → agent types, roles, interaction protocols (often FIPA-style), and shared data; an agent acquaintance model.
- **Detailed agent design** → per agent-type: capabilities, plans (a plan is a context-scoped recipe for a goal, often with a fallback recipe), beliefs (belief-base entries), and event handlers.
- **Implementation** → mapping onto a BDI platform (e.g. Jadex): plans to plan bodies, beliefs to belief-base entries, protocols to message handlers.
- **Testing** → unit tests on plans/capabilities, integration tests on protocols, and where formal methods apply, model-checking key properties such as protocol liveness and safety invariants on shared data.
- **Maintenance** → on a requirement shift, walk the chain requirement → goal → role → agent → plan, updating each artefact in step.

The traceability chain *is* the deliverable. Ad-hoc patches that bypass the chain destroy the very property the heavyweight process was bought for; the deployed system silently stops matching its own architecture.

steps.name: Requirements analysis
steps.description: Capture stakeholder goals, scenarios, and what the environment is like. Produce a goal hierarchy. Break big goals into sub-goals, all the way down to leaves you can actually act on.

steps.name: System specification
steps.description: Pin down the edge of the system. What the agents can sense, what they can do, and which scenarios tie goals to sequences of actions. Produce a system overview diagram.

steps.name: Architectural design
steps.description: Group the goals into agent types. Assign roles. Spell out how the agents talk to each other. Identify the shared data and say where it lives. Produce a map of which agents know each other, plus the protocols.

steps.name: Detailed agent design
steps.description: For each agent type, design what it can do, its plans, its beliefs, and how it handles events. A plan is a recipe for reaching a goal in a given situation. Beliefs are the agent's picture of the world.

steps.name: Implementation
steps.description: Turn the detailed designs into running code on the chosen agent platform. Map plans to the platform's plan bodies, beliefs to belief-base entries, and protocols to message handlers.

steps.name: Testing
steps.description: Test single plans and abilities on their own. Test how the agents interact against the protocols. Where formal methods fit, model-check key properties, such as whether a protocol always makes progress or whether shared data stays safe.

steps.name: Maintenance
steps.description: When requirements change, walk the change back up the chain: requirement, then goal, then role, then agent, then plan. Update the documents at each step. The trail is the asset. Quick patches that skip the chain destroy it.

principles: Every phase produces a named document. A phase with no document is a wish, not a result.
The main deliverable is the trail from a stakeholder goal to the plan that runs in production.
What each agent believes, wants, and plans to do are first-class parts of the design, not implementation details.
Maintenance walks the chain. Patching code without updating the documents upstream destroys the very trail you paid for.

known_failure_modes.note: Lifecycle stalls in detailed design forever because the team treats the artefacts as the deliverable instead of as a path to running code.
Translating a flawed manual workflow directly into a goal hierarchy bakes the flaw into every downstream agent.
Maintenance patches the implementation without updating goals and roles — over time the deployed system stops matching its own architecture.

sources.title: Prometheus: A Methodology for Developing Intelligent Agents (Padgham & Winikoff, AAMAS'02 AOSE Workshop)
sources.quote: The Prometheus methodology consists of three phases ... The system specification phase focuses on identifying the basic functionalities of the system, along with inputs (percepts), outputs (actions) and any important shared data sources. The architectural design phase uses the outputs from the previous phase to determine which agents the system will contain and how they will interact. The detailed design phase looks at the internals of each agent and how it will accomplish its tasks within the overall system.
